title: The Jains
excerpt: Paul Dundas's authoritative introduction to the Jain religion, covering its history, doctrines, practices, literature, and contemporary significance in both India and the diaspora.
keyIdeas: Jainism is one of the world's oldest living religions, with a continuous history of over 2,500 years
The central Jain doctrines of ahimsa (non-violence), anekantavada (many-sidedness), and aparigraha (non-attachment) form a coherent philosophical system
Jainism has maintained its distinct identity despite centuries of interaction with Hindu and Buddhist traditions
keyTakeaways: Jain cosmology describes an eternal universe with no creator god, governed by karmic law
The Jain path to liberation requires rigorous asceticism, particularly for monks and nuns
Jain literature includes an extensive canon of scriptures written in Prakrit and Sanskrit
The laity supports the monastic community through donations and observes less strict versions of the vows
Jainism has adapted to modernity through educational institutions, diaspora communities, and social reform
whoShouldRead: Students of Indian religions seeking authoritative coverage of Jainism
Scholars of comparative religion interested in non-theistic traditions
Anyone practicing or studying ahimsa (non-violence) as a spiritual principle
Readers interested in how ancient traditions adapt to modernity
whoShouldSkip: Those looking for a popular, devotional introduction to Jain practice
Readers wanting a simple overview — Dundas is scholarly and detailed
People seeking comparative analysis with other religions rather than Jainism itself
